text: Laparoscopic right hemicolectomy in an obese patient of BMI of fifty. Our case is a seventy four year old female with body mass index of fifty. A colonoscopy done for anaemia detected a transverse colon cancer which was tattooed. Staging CT revealed this to be on the right side of the transverse colon. An open approach, although less difficult as compared to a laparoscopic approach, would mean a longer post-operative recovery time and a higher risk of wound complications. Hence, a laparoscopic approach, though challenging, was considered.

The surgeon gains access using extra-long laparoscopic ports: a 12mm port at the umbilicus with an open technique, a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa, an 11mm port below the umbilicus on the left side, a 5mm port above the umbilicus, and an additional port above the umbilicus. The procedure is performed using standard laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle using traction and counter-traction techniques. A hook diathermy is employed to open a plane below the pedicle, dividing it with the aid of a harmonic device. The convex end of the harmonic device is used for blunt dissection above the duodenum. A tonsil swab is placed anterior and inferior to the duodenum to guide further dissection. The tattooed transverse colon cancer is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to create and enlarge a window in the omentum, making it taut and dissecting it off the transverse colon. The dissection progresses laterally towards the swab placed in the infracolic compartment, anterior to the duodenum, which is visible below the swab. The dissection continues laterally to take down the hepatic flexure area.

The surgeon uses extra long graspers to aid in dissection over a long intra-abdominal distance. The hepatic flexure is fully mobilized, followed by lateral mobilization of the colon. Dense adhesions between the cecum, ascending colon, and lateral abdominal wall are released using gentle diathermy and a harmonic device. The middle colic pedicles are dissected with the harmonic device and secured with hemolog clips before division. The transverse colon is divided distal to the tattooed site of the cancer.

The transverse colon distal to the cancer is prepared for division. An endogia stapler is used to securely divide the transverse colon at the tumor site. The entire specimen is visible after the division, and the right colon is fully mobilized.

Once the ports were removed, a picture has been taken to indicate the habitus of the patient. In the post-operative period, the patient was taken to the high dependency unit to stay overnight as a result of the comorbidities. She was started on a normal diet on day one post-op and discharged by day six. Histology showed a complete R-zero rejection was achieved.
